summary: This is a methodologically sound negative result paper with one strong positive finding. The core claim---that co-occurrence clustering cannot detect hierarchical feature absorption due to token-level mutual exclusivity---is theoretically grounded and empirically supported. The collision rate proxy validation (r=0.869) adds constructive value. However, there are critical issues around ground truth definition circularity, small sample size, terminology confusion, and several overclaims that need correction before submission.

findings.description: Ground truth definition is circular and potentially too permissive. The paper defines 'true absorption' as Jaccard overlap of top-K features, then validates 'collision rate' (same quantity) as a proxy for 'true absorption.' This is circular: collision rate IS the ground truth definition, not an independent proxy. The pilot summary explicitly notes this concern: 'feature 11746 dominates all 26 letters, suggesting a first-letter super-feature rather than distributed absorption.' The paper never addresses whether the GT definition overcounts absorption.
findings.suggestion: Explicitly acknowledge the circularity in the GT definition. Distinguish between (a) the operational definition used for GT labeling and (b) the independent validation that collision rate correlates with this definition. Add a paragraph discussing the feature 11746 dominance concern and why the correlation is still meaningful despite this limitation.

findings.description: Only 7 ground truth absorption pairs for UAD evaluation. With 7 positives and 4155 detected candidates, statistical power is extremely limited. The F1=0.00048 figure has huge variance---a single additional true positive would change F1 by ~40%. The paper presents this as definitive when it is actually based on a tiny sample.
findings.suggestion: Add confidence intervals or bootstrap estimates for the F1 score. Acknowledge that the UAD evaluation is based on only 7 ground truth pairs and frame the conclusion as 'UAD fails on this test set' rather than 'UAD fails universally.' Consider running the evaluation on the 56 pairs from the collision correlation (with appropriate thresholding) to expand the ground truth.

findings.description: The paper claims 'absorption features are mutually exclusive at the token level' as a universal property, but this is only demonstrated for number words in a synthetic sequence. The evidence comes from a single constructed example ('one two three four five six seven eight'). This is not general evidence---it is a cherry-picked example. Real-world hierarchical concepts (e.g., 'animal' and 'dog') DO co-occur in natural text ('the dog is an animal').
findings.suggestion: Soften the universal claim. State that mutual exclusivity holds for the tested hierarchies (numbers, punctuation) where child concepts are token-disjoint, but acknowledge that semantic hierarchies (animal/dog) may show different patterns. Test on at least one semantic hierarchy or add this as a stated limitation.

findings.description: Terminology confusion between 'collision rate' and 'true absorption rate.' The paper uses 'collision rate' to mean Jaccard overlap of top-K features (Section 3.4), but also defines 'true absorption rate' as the SAME quantity (Section 3.2). In the Results, 'collision rate vs true absorption rate' is presented as validating a proxy, but they are the same metric computed on the same data. This is not proxy validation---it is self-correlation.
findings.suggestion: Clarify the distinction: define 'true absorption rate' using Chanin et al.'s original probe-based method (or acknowledge that the operational definition is an approximation), and 'collision rate' as the Jaccard overlap. If both are Jaccard overlap, reframe as 'collision rate reliably recovers the operational absorption definition' rather than 'validates a proxy.'

findings.description: The K-means ablation achieves 85.7% recall (6/7 TP) but the paper dismisses it as 'still failing.' This is inconsistent---85.7% recall is actually quite good for an unsupervised method. The issue is precision (0.043%), but the paper does not discuss whether the precision problem is addressable (e.g., by raising a similarity threshold post-clustering). Dismissing the best-performing variant without exploring why it succeeds where others fail is a missed opportunity.
findings.suggestion: Add analysis of why K-means succeeds where Ward linkage fails. Does K-means happen to group features by some non-co-occurrence property? Could a post-processing threshold on cluster coherence improve precision? This would strengthen the paper's constructive contribution.

findings.description: The abstract claims 'F1 = 0.0005, indistinguishable from random sampling (same-cluster random F1 = 0.00048).' These values are identical to 4 significant figures, but the paper presents this as a measured finding rather than a mathematical identity. With 1 TP out of 4155 detected pairs and 1 TP out of 4155 same-cluster random pairs, the F1 values MUST be identical by construction---both have exactly 1 TP, 4154 FP, 6 FN. This is not an empirical finding; it is arithmetic.
findings.suggestion: Reframe: 'UAD detects exactly 1 true positive out of 4155 candidates. Randomly sampling the same number of pairs from within clusters yields the same 1 true positive by chance. UAD's sophisticated pipeline provides zero improvement over this trivial baseline.' This is more honest and avoids presenting an identity as a statistical finding.

findings.description: The novelty check was performed on the ORIGINAL UAD proposal (positive result framing), not the current negative result paper. The novelty assessment states 'UAD as the first unsupervised absorption detection method' is novel, but the current paper is about why UAD FAILS. The novelty of 'UAD doesn't work' is different from 'UAD is a new method.' The paper needs to re-assess novelty for its actual contribution: falsifying co-occurrence clustering for absorption detection.
findings.suggestion: Reframe novelty claims. The actual novel contributions are: (1) first systematic empirical evaluation of co-occurrence clustering for absorption detection, (2) identification of token-level mutual exclusivity as a structural barrier, (3) validation of collision rate as a proxy (at this scale). These are methodological observations, not new methods.

findings.description: The paper tests UAD on only one SAE (gpt2-small-res-jb, layer 8) with 1,000 sequences. The claim that 'the root cause is architecture-independent' (Section 2.2, Proposal) is unsupported. Different SAE architectures (TopK, Gated, JumpReLU) may learn different feature structures. The conclusion that 'more SAEs would not change the conclusion' is an assertion, not evidence.
findings.suggestion: Add at least one additional SAE test (e.g., a different layer or a TopK SAE) or explicitly state this as a limitation. The claim about architecture-independence should be downgraded from assertion to hypothesis.

findings.description: The paper introduces 'collision rate' in the abstract without definition. Readers encountering this term for the first time will not understand what it means. The term is also non-standard in SAE literature (novelty check confirms this).
findings.suggestion: Add a brief parenthetical definition in the abstract: 'collision rate (Jaccard overlap of top-K activating features)'. Also add a footnote or sentence noting that this is a novel operational definition introduced in this paper.

findings.description: The paper mentions 'Code and data are available in the supplementary materials' but there is no evidence that code has been prepared for release. The experiments use custom implementations of UAD/DFDA that are not linked or described in sufficient detail for independent reproduction.
findings.suggestion: Add a reproducibility statement with a link to code repository (even if placeholder). Include pseudocode for the UAD pipeline and collision rate computation in the Methods section.

findings.description: Single seed (42) used throughout. While token-level mutual exclusivity is deterministic, the specific features identified (11513, 24189, etc.) and the clustering results may vary with different corpus samples or seeds. No sensitivity analysis is provided.
findings.suggestion: Add a brief sensitivity analysis: run UAD with 2-3 additional seeds and report variance in F1. If variance is low, this strengthens the conclusion. If high, it reveals fragility.

findings.description: The paper's tone oscillates between 'harsh rejection of co-occurrence' and 'constructive forward look.' In some places (Conclusion), it calls on the community to 'abandon co-occurrence-based approaches'---this is stronger than the evidence supports, since the paper only tested one type of co-occurrence clustering (UAD's specific pipeline) on one type of hierarchy.
findings.suggestion: Soften the universal rejection language. Instead of 'abandon co-occurrence-based approaches,' say 'co-occurrence clustering as implemented in UAD is unsuitable for detecting token-disjoint hierarchical absorption.' Leave open the possibility that modified co-occurrence methods could work for semantic hierarchies where children co-occur.

findings.description: The F5 false positive analysis claims '99.98% false positive rate' but this is computed as FP/(TP+FP) = 4154/4155. This is the standard definition of false positive RATE (1 - precision), but the term 'false positive rate' in ML typically means FP/(FP+TN), which would be much lower. Using non-standard terminology without clarification could confuse readers.
findings.suggestion: Use standard terminology: report 'precision = 0.024%' rather than 'false positive rate = 99.98%.' If using the latter, explicitly define it as 1 - precision to avoid confusion with the standard FPR definition.

findings.description: The ablation results show that 'no dead filter' and 'no phi filter' produce IDENTICAL results to full UAD (same TP/FP/FN counts). This is suspicious---removing filtering steps should change the candidate set. The identical counts suggest these filtering steps are no-ops in this implementation (e.g., no features were actually filtered out). The paper does not explain this.
findings.suggestion: Investigate and report: how many features were actually removed by dead feature filtering? How many pairs were removed by phi filtering? If the filters removed nothing, explain why (e.g., all 500 selected features passed the variance threshold). This is important for interpreting the ablation results.

findings.description: The paper claims 'Our work establishes both a negative result that prevents the community from pursuing a dead-end direction' (Proposal Abstract). This overstates the impact. The paper tests one specific method (UAD) on one specific SAE with one specific type of hierarchy. It does not establish that the entire direction is a dead end---only that this specific approach fails in this specific setting.
findings.suggestion: Soften impact claims. 'Our work demonstrates that UAD's co-occurrence clustering approach fails for token-disjoint hierarchical absorption, suggesting the community should explore alternative approaches rather than tuning UAD hyperparameters.'

metric_gaming_risks: Collision rate and 'true absorption rate' are the same metric (Jaccard overlap of top-K features), making the correlation validation circular rather than independent
F1 = 0.00048 vs same-cluster random F1 = 0.00048 is a mathematical identity (same TP/FP/FN counts), not an empirical statistical finding
The 7 ground truth pairs are too few for reliable F1 estimation; a single TP difference would change results dramatically
K-means achieves 85.7% recall but is dismissed without exploring why it outperforms other variants
The token-level mutual exclusivity evidence is a single cherry-picked example (number sequence), not systematic evidence across hierarchies
novelty_risks: Novelty check was performed on the original positive-result framing, not the current negative-result paper. The actual contribution is methodological observation, not a new method
The core insight (co-occurrence clustering cannot detect mutually exclusive features) is logically trivial once stated---the value is in the empirical demonstration, not the insight itself
Collision rate as a proxy has been 'mentioned in passing' by Chanin et al. (2024); the paper's validation at n=56 is incremental, not transformative
Reviewer may question whether a paper about 'method X fails' is sufficient contribution without also proposing and validating a working alternative
reproducibility_gaps: Custom UAD implementation details insufficient for independent reproduction
Single SAE, single layer, single model---no cross-validation
Ground truth pairs manually identified without explicit criteria or annotation protocol
No pre-registration of analysis plan (hypotheses were documented but not pre-registered before data collection)
